plea_details: Richard Fanne Gildert, along with John Ingledesby, Walter Fetplace, and Michael Luke, is accused of falsely and maliciously procuring an indictment against John Patyn (also known as John Payn), Gabriel Fleming (also known as Gabriel Flomyng), and Robert Basset. The indictment, taken on January 20, 34 Henry VI at Hillingdon, Middlesex, before Justices of the Peace, falsely accused them of assaulting Walter John with force and arms (swords, staves, bows and arrows, lances, langdebeves, jacks, sallets, briganders, and other defensive weapons) against the King's peace. John Patyn was residing in London at the time of the indictment, not Middlesex. This action is brought under a statute of 8 Henry VI, which allows those falsely indicted and acquitted to recover triple damages from the procurers.

plea_details: The defendants, Thomas Hawe, John Hawe, and Alan Hore, arrayed in a warlike manner with swords, staves, cloaks, jacks, saddles, lances, and bills of war, forcibly entered and held four acres of land, meadow, and pasture belonging to Thomas Greswold. Thomas Greswold and his predecessors had been peacefully seised of this land for 200 years or more. They also drained a pond, evacuating the water and fish (20 lampreys, 20 bream, 20 tench, and 200 roach) valued at 100 shillings, and cut a bolt from a pipe worth twenty pence, taking it. Furthermore, they assaulted Thomas Greswold, a King's peacekeeper and justice, in his house while he was dining with Lady Isabella Merrell and other women, shooting arrows at him and causing great fear.

plea_details: Defendants, along with other unknown wrongdoers, incited and abetted by Thomas Hawe, forcibly entered and held 4 acres of land, meadow, and pasture belonging to Thomas Greswold, against the statute concerning forcible entries. Armed with swords, staves, bows, arrows, axes, helmets, sallets, lances, and bills of war, they also removed Greswold's drainpipe, emptied his pond of water and fish (20 pike, 20 bream, 20 tench, and 200 roach, valued at 100 shillings), cut and stole the pipe's valve (valued at 20 pence). They then proceeded to Greswold's house, a Justice of the Peace, and assaulted him while he was at supper. Despite Greswold identifying himself as a JP and ordering them to keep the peace, they assaulted him again, shooting many arrows and causing a great and terrible affray.
full_text: Rex Hore Alan Hawe Thomas Blakedon Solihull Defendants, along with other unknown wrongdoers, incited and abetted by Thomas Hawe, forcibly entered and held 4 acres of land, meadow, and pasture belonging to Thomas Greswold, against the statute concerning forcible entries. Armed with swords, staves, bows, arrows, axes, helmets, sallets, lances, and bills of war, they also removed Greswold's drainpipe, emptied his pond of water and fish (20 pike, 20 bream, 20 tench, and 200 roach, valued at 100 shillings), cut and stole the pipe's valve (valued at 20 pence). They then proceeded to Greswold's house, a Justice of the Peace, and assaulted him while he was at supper. Despite Greswold identifying himself as a JP and ordering them to keep the peace, they assaulted him again, shooting many arrows and causing a great and terrible affray.

plea_details: The defendants forcibly entered John Wale's land in Sproxton, constructed and compelled a granary, and took four cartloads of rye/grain (valued at 20 shillings) feloniously. They detained the grain by force in Brikelesworth. They prevented Thomas Shyreff, a King's bailiff, from replevying the grain at John Wale's suit, threatening him. Later, when the undersheriff Robert Ense attempted to replevy the grain, the defendants, before his arrival, threshed the grain at night and feloniously carried, embezzled, and removed it outside the county limits. These actions were against the form of the statute concerning illicit conventicles, riots, routes, and insurrections, as well as against the King's peace and statutes concerning replevin, causing John Wale 40 pounds in damages.

plea_details: The new foundation, erection, and continued maintenance of a 'hamme' (weir/sluice) with a 'gurgite' (weir/fish-garth) in the River Thames near Rotecote bridge in Faringdon. This structure obstructs the river, causing excessive water levels and flooding of the royal highway from Faringdon to Rotecote, and inundating lands, meadows, and pastures belonging to the Abbot of Beaulieu Regis in Faringdon, and lands in Eton and Buscote, and other free tenants adjacent to the river. This has led to damage, danger, and loss of property for royal subjects traversing the highway, and diminished profits for landowners. It also significantly hinders the common passage of the King's lieges by boat, contrary to statute and to the contempt of the King and nuisance of the whole country.

plea_details: John Gorseley of Boldington, yeoman, on 9 August in the 39th year of the king's reign, at Boldington, with force and arms (swords, staffs, etc.) made an assault upon John Yery, servant of the Prior of Newnham, beat, wounded, and ill-treated him, by which the Prior lost the service of John Yery, to the damage of John Yery and the Prior. And that the said Gorseley on 1 June in the 38th year of the king's reign, with force and arms (swords, staffs, etc.) made an assault upon Richard Wayte at Goldington, and entered the Prior's warren and there hunted with his dogs, by virtue of which chase two hares worth 10s of the Prior's goods and chattels died, to the great damage of the Prior. And that the said John Gorseley with force and arms on 1 June in the 37th year of the king's reign, at Goldington in a pasture called Ravenesbregge, with force and arms (swords, staffs, etc.) made an assault upon John Chestre, servant of the Prior, beat and ill-treated him, by which the Prior lost the service of John Chestre, and chased 12 bullocks of the Prior's goods and chattels there and then, and against the King's peace.

plea_details: Action based on an 8 Henry VI statute. The defendants falsely and maliciously procured an indictment against William Derlyngton for violently assaulting and feloniously raping Margaret Michell in Middlesex. plea_details: A presentment was made by 12 jurors that John Lobenham, William Staldewell, John Bussh, William Saldewell, and 40 unknown accomplices, did unlawfully assemble and arm themselves in a warlike manner, riding in illicit routs, terrifying the King's lieges. They assaulted John Wale and William Wale at Sprotton, threatening them to the point they despaired of their lives. They then forcibly entered John Wale's cultivated land, trampled his rye, and feloniously took and carried away four wagonloads of rye (worth 20 shillings). They detained the grain and resisted the King's bailiff, Thomas Shyres, and later the undersheriff, Robert Olney, who attempted to replevy the grain, by threats of death and rescue. The defendants then threshed the grain by night and feloniously carried it out of the county, in a conspiracy to maintain their wrongdoing and subvert the King's laws.

plea_details: King seeks to levy fines against William Daynecourt and his sureties (John Santon and William Parchomener) for breach of a peace bond. Daynecourt, previously in Marshalsea prison, was released on recognizance (bail) to keep the peace, especially towards Alice Porons. He allegedly breached this bond by assaulting Johanna Payn, taking 10 marks from her, and by taking two silver-bound murras from John Scotte, all 'vi et armis'. Daynecourt and his sureties had acknowledged debts of 40 pounds (Daynecourt) and 20 pounds (each surety) to the King if the peace was broken.

plea_details: Walter Bury and John Saverey, with force and arms (swords, staves, etc.), broke the closes of Nicholas Kynghton and Thomas Facy at Durerste and Kentysbeare. They also, with certain animals (horses, oxen, cows, pigs, and sheep), depastured, trampled, and consumed their grass recently growing at Durerste, to the value of 100 shillings. This trespass of depasturing, trampling, and consuming the grass continued from the Thursday next after the feast of Saints Peter and Paul in the 37th year of the present King's reign up to the third day of January in the 38th year of the King's reign (the day of the original writ's impetration), to the grave damage of the plaintiffs and against the King's peace.
